title: A Short History of Nearly Everything

chapters.title: The building block of life
chapters.text:
Your life depends on trillions of atoms arranged in a delicate pattern. For you to stay alive, these atoms must hold their places, stacked in just the right way.

We still do not have a clear answer for why this arrangement exists. What pulled these lifeless, unconscious, indifferent atoms together in the pattern that is you? They feel nothing and have nothing to gain or lose.

And yet you are made of trillions of them. Mindless particles come together in a balance that makes you unique. That balance, however, is wobbly by nature. A well spent life lasts around six hundred fifty thousand hours. Once that time is up, the atoms lose interest and pull the whole structure down without a second thought.

We should be grateful they agreed to take part in the first place. The ingredients of a human body exist on other planets, but those atoms have not chosen to make humans anywhere else. Break a person down into elements and you find carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, calcium, and a bit of sulfur.

The particular combination of atoms that makes you has appeared only once in the history of the universe, which is what makes you, you.

Whether or not atoms form life elsewhere, the fact stays the same: atoms make up everything. They are the raw material of air, water, clouds, planets, rocks, stars, and swirling clouds of gas in space. Everything you know about the universe is built from atoms. We tend to forget that because they are everywhere.

No law keeps the universe in order, and no rule tells it what to do. The universe runs itself. And yet there was a time when there was no universe at all. There was nothing.

This is a topic worth a closer look. If you want to understand how we got here, this summary will give you plenty to chew on and leave you even more amazed at the world we live in.

chapters.title: Putting a universe together
chapters.text:
A proton is far too small for the human mind to picture. To build a universe, though, you have to shrink that proton to a billionth of its size, then squeeze it into a tiny space so it looks big again. Drop in about an ounce of matter and you have an expanding universe.

Bill Bryson has written that protons give an atom its identity and electrons give it its personality.

A more traditional universe takes a few extra steps. First, gather all the matter in the cosmos and squeeze it into a point with no size and no dimensions. That is what physicists call a singularity. If you manage to pull that off, step back fast, because a Big Bang is coming.

Many people picture a singularity as a dot floating in a dark void. In reality, there is nothing around it. There is no way to know how long it has been there. Time has no meaning at a singularity.

And so, from nothing, our universe begins.

There is no single point you can point to and say the universe started here.

The singularity appeared in a single blinding flash. In an instant it came into being. Words cannot describe how quickly it happened. In that same moment, every law of physics was born.

At the start, the universe was intensely hot. That energy created the light elements: hydrogen, helium, and a trace of lithium. In about three minutes, matter itself came into existence. Next time you make a sandwich, which takes about three minutes, remember that physics built the raw material of the universe in roughly the same stretch of time.

It is worth noting that, broken down, the Earth is about thirty two percent iron, thirty percent oxygen, fifteen percent silicon, and fourteen percent magnesium.

chapters.title: What we know is a tiny drop
chapters.text:
Astronomers do remarkable work. If someone struck a match on the moon, an astronomer on Earth would know about it. They study the universe by watching the faint flicker of distant stars, and from that they can estimate the size and possible habitability of planets light years away.

With radio telescopes, they pick up traces of radiation so faint that it is hard to believe they can be detected at all. Even so, it took astronomers centuries to confirm that a moon orbits Pluto. Despite all their tools, they only found it about fifty years ago.

To be fair to them, light from that moon takes about seven hours to reach Earth. Since faster than light travel is still science fiction, it is hard to imagine how long a real trip out there would take.

Bill Bryson has said that physics is a search for ultimate simplicity, and so far we have only an elegant kind of mess.

Many people wonder why it took so long. The answer is simple. Astronomers can do astonishing things, but they can only point their instruments in one direction at a time.

On a true scale map where the Earth is the size of a pea, Pluto would sit about two and a half kilometers away.

Space is vast and mostly empty. Our own solar system is probably the most active patch in the whole universe. Everything we can see, from the Sun to the planets, the moons, the asteroid belt, and other drifting debris, takes up less than a trillionth of the space out there. That is why every familiar map of the universe is wildly out of scale.

chapters.title: A wager that shaped physics
chapters.text:
Edmond Halley lived a life packed with achievement. He served as professor of geometry at Oxford and later as Astronomer Royal. He was not just a scholar, though. His love of adventure led him to become a sea captain, where he put his talent for mapping to work.

Halley never stopped tinkering with new ideas. He built an early deep sea diving bell, opened up new ways to explore underwater, and came up with the weather map and the actuarial table, tools that changed both meteorology and finance. He also worked out ways to estimate the age of the Earth and the distance from the Earth to the Sun, mixing geology with astronomy. On top of all that, he even thought about how to keep fish fresh out of season, showing the practical side of his genius.

One of his most famous moves was a bet. In 1683, he was dining in London with two other thinkers, Robert Hooke and Christopher Wren. They began arguing about why the planets move in ellipses. Wren offered forty shillings to whoever could settle the question.

No one collected the prize. Hooke claimed he had the answer but refused to share it. Curious, Halley traveled to Cambridge to see Isaac Newton, hoping Newton could solve the puzzle.

The two met in August 1684. Halley proposed a thought experiment to Newton: if a planet attraction to the Sun weakened with the square of its distance, what shape would the orbit take? Newton answered almost at once: an ellipse. He had worked it out before but had lost the calculations.

That meeting changed history. Halley pushed Newton to redo the work and write it up properly. The result was far more than a short paper. Newton produced his masterwork, the Principia, which transformed physics. That book, with its laws of motion and universal gravitation, made Newton famous.

Everything in the universe pulls on everything else. Every object exerts a gravitational tug on its neighbors.

At the core of the Principia are Newton three laws of motion and the universal law of gravitation.

Newton laws of motion are these. An object pushed by a force moves in the direction of that force and keeps going in a straight line until something else changes its path. For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. And the law of universal gravitation describes how every mass in the universe attracts every other mass.

chapters.title: A breakthrough born of pure thought
chapters.text:
Albert Einstein did not have an easy start. He did not speak until he was three, and the family moved often because of his father business troubles. He was a bright student, but no one thought of him as exceptional during his school years.

Patience matters, because even geniuses need time to grow.

Einstein graduated from the Zurich Polytechnic in 1900 and published papers in the Annalen der Physik. His first paper tackled the same kind of problem as Max Planck quantum theory. Between 1902 and 1904, he worked on statistical mechanics, although his work overlapped with that of J. Willard Gibbs.

In 1901, Einstein fell hard for a fellow student. They had a child together and placed the child for adoption. After two years of dating, they married, but Einstein never saw that child again.

In 1902, Einstein took a job at a patent office. The work was mentally engaging but did not drain him, which left him free to think about physics. It was there, in 1905, that he worked out the theory that would change how we see time, space, and gravity: the theory of relativity.

The paper was called On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies. It was unusual: it had no references, no math beyond the basics, and thanked just one person, Michele Besso.

chapters.title: Matter and energy are two sides of the same coin
chapters.text:
On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies did not include the famous mass energy equivalence. That came in a short follow up paper, which revealed how much energy is locked inside every object.

The equation E equals mc squared laid bare a basic truth of the universe: matter and energy are not separate things, but two faces of the same reality. Energy is matter set free, and matter is energy waiting to be released. The c squared term tells you just how much energy is packed into matter.

This idea lit up old puzzles. It explained why uranium keeps releasing energy over time, and why stars keep shining for billions of years.

You are a powerful generator of energy, so learn to direct it toward goals that matter to you.

Around 1907, Einstein saw a workman fall from a roof. The sight started him thinking about gravity. He began to ask how gravity fit with what he had already figured out. His theory assumed bodies moving through empty space, and now he had to think about what happens when something like gravity gets in the way. The problem stayed with him for more than ten years. The answer appeared in his next major paper.

chapters.title: Wrapping it up
chapters.text:
Humans rose to the top of life on Earth remarkably quickly when you zoom out on the planet timeline. We have been here for the briefest blink of geological time, and the fact that we have lasted this long is a mix of luck and good breaks. Our survival is a tribute to how flexible and tough our species is. But we also need to take more responsibility and think harder about how our choices shape what comes next.

Every living thing, from the tiniest microbe to humans, is a small miracle in a cold and mostly empty universe. Out of all of them, only humans have the chance to leave a thoughtful, careful mark on our one home.

That knowledge comes with a sense of privilege. We do not just exist. We can notice the world and make it better. We are only beginning to understand that gift and put it to work.

The exciting part is that we are only at the start. We have to make sure there is an end worth reaching. That will need a lot more than luck.

A few things you can try.

Take in the world around you and let your mind drift.

You do not need to be a genius to understand the workings of the Earth. Pay closer attention to your surroundings and stay curious about what you see.

Wake up the part of you that asks questions, and notice how interesting science can be.

If you read all the way to the end, well done.
